coredata.dc:title: Structural and fluorescence studies of B,N-codoped carbon dot@UiO-66 MOF nanocomposites for rapid and selective detection of cefixime in biological and environmental samples

coredata.dc:description: This study reports the development of a novel and sensitive fluorescence sensor for the detection of cefixime (CFX), a widely used antibiotic. The sensor is based on a highly stable B,N-codoped carbon dot@UiO-66 metal-organic framework (MOF) nanocomposite, which exhibits a BET surface area of 35.63 m²/g, a pore volume of 0.03105 cm³/g, and an average pore size of 3.49 nm, consistent with mesoporosity. ImageJ analysis revealed polyhedral-like particles ranging from 5.85 to 22.8 nm, with an average size of 15.3 nm, aligning with BET results. FE-SEM analysis confirmed a well-dispersed, heterogeneous structure, where B,N-CD nanoparticles are uniformly distributed over the UiO-66 MOF framework. EDS analysis validated the presence of C (19.65 wt.%), O (36.46 wt.%), Zr (31.71 wt.%), B (3.63 wt.%), and N (8.54 wt.%). XPS further confirmed successful element incorporation. The fluorescence of the nanocomposite was effectively quenched by CFX via static quenching and inner filter effects. The sensor exhibited a low detection limit (LOD) of 4.21 nM (1.907 ng/mL), a quenching constant of 0.0842–3.51 M⁻¹, and a wide linear range (0–100 nM). It demonstrated high selectivity for CFX over other antibiotics and metal ions. The sensor retained stability, with only a 2.23 % fluorescence intensity decrease after 60 days at 12 °C, and maintained performance over multiple cycles. Recovery rates of 96.9 %–101.63 % in real-world samples (honey, tap water, seawater) further validated its applicability. These findings highlight the potential of B,N-CD@UiO-66 as a reliable sensor for CFX detection in biological and environmental matrices.

head.ce:title: Structural and fluorescence studies of B,N-codoped carbon dot@UiO-66 MOF nanocomposites for rapid and selective detection of cefixime in biological and environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymeric Materials Research Laboratory, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Arts and Science, TR North Cyprus, Eastern Mediterranean University, Famagusta via Mersin 10, Türkiye
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymeric Materials Research Laboratory, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Arts and Science, TR North Cyprus, Eastern Mediterranean University, Famagusta via Mersin 10, Türkiye

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the development of a novel and sensitive fluorescence sensor for the detection of cefixime (CFX), a widely used antibiotic. The sensor is based on a highly stable B,N-codoped carbon dot@UiO-66 metal-organic framework (MOF) nanocomposite, which exhibits a BET surface area of 35.63 m²/g, a pore volume of 0.03105 cm³/g, and an average pore size of 3.49 nm, consistent with mesoporosity. ImageJ analysis revealed polyhedral-like particles ranging from 5.85 to 22.8 nm, with an average size of 15.3 nm, aligning with BET results. FE-SEM analysis confirmed a well-dispersed, heterogeneous structure, where B,N-CD nanoparticles are uniformly distributed over the UiO-66 MOF framework. EDS analysis validated the presence of C (19.65 wt.%), O (36.46 wt.%), Zr (31.71 wt.%), B (3.63 wt.%), and N (8.54 wt.%). XPS further confirmed successful element incorporation. The fluorescence of the nanocomposite was effectively quenched by CFX via static quenching and inner filter effects. The sensor exhibited a low detection limit (LOD) of 4.21 nM (1.907 ng/mL), a quenching constant of 0.0842–3.51 M⁻¹, and a wide linear range (0–100 nM). It demonstrated high selectivity for CFX over other antibiotics and metal ions. The sensor retained stability, with only a 2.23 % fluorescence intensity decrease after 60 days at 12 °C, and maintained performance over multiple cycles. Recovery rates of 96.9 %–101.63 % in real-world samples (honey, tap water, seawater) further validated its applicability. These findings highlight the potential of B,N-CD@UiO-66 as a reliable sensor for CFX detection in biological and environmental matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cefixime, a widely used third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic, is extensively employed in both human and veterinary medicine to treat bacterial infections such as otitis media, strep throat, pneumonia, urinary tract infections, gonorrhea, and Lyme disease. Its broad-spectrum application has led to widespread global consumption. Market analyses (Verified Market Reports and Volza) estimate that the global cefixime market was valued at approximately USD 2.3 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach USD 3.9 billion by 2032, growing at a compound annual growth rate of 5.5 %. These figures highlight the extensive use of cefixime in clinical practice and underscore the need for sensitive detection methods, particularly given growing concerns about its environmental and health impacts [1–3]. The release of cefixime into the environment, primarily through wastewater discharge and agricultural runoff, promotes antibiotic resistance in bacteria, disrupts natural microbial ecosystems, and poses potential ecological hazards. Additionally, cefixime residues in food and water can contribute to increased antibiotic resistance in human pathogens and may trigger allergic reactions. Therefore, the development of highly sensitive and selective analytical methods for cefixime detection is crucial for monitoring its presence and mitigating associated risks.

Conventional analytical techniques like high-performance liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays have been used for cefixime detection [4–6]. However, these methods often suffer from limitations, including complex and expensive instrumentation, time-consuming procedures, challenges in detecting low concentrations in complex matrices, and lack of portability. These limitations necessitate the development of alternative analytical methods that are rapid, sensitive, and selective [7]. Fluorescence-based approaches have emerged as promising alternatives due to their simplicity, low cost, rapid response, and high sensitivity [8–11]. Various fluorescent materials, including semiconductor quantum dots, carbon-based quantum dots, noble metal-based nanoclusters, and fluorescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), have been explored for sensing applications [12,13]. These materials offer varying degrees of success in addressing the need for rapid, sensitive, and selective detection methods, contributing to the development of effective monitoring and mitigation strategies for antibiotic residues.

MOFs, characterized by large surface areas, high porosities, tunable structures, and abundant functional sites, have emerged as promising candidates for sensing applications [14,15]. For instance, Shi et al. [16] recently reported the synthesis of luminescent dye@MOF platforms for sensing metronidazole and dimetazole antibiotics, achieving limits of detection (LODs) of 0.58 µM and 0.64 µM, respectively. The authors observed that the presence of these antibiotics resulted in significant quenching of the dye@MOF fluorescence, demonstrating excellent sensing performance with high sensitivity. Similarly, Li et al. [17] developed a sensitive ratiometric luminescent sensing platform for tetracycline and levofloxacin antibiotics by encapsulating fluorescein sodium within Zr-based UiO-66 MOF. This platform demonstrated detection limits of 0.2444 µM and 0.2808 µM for tetracycline and levofloxacin, respectively, through fluorescence quenching and enhancement of the fluorescence emission peak.

Despite their impressive fluorescence sensing performance, many MOFs exhibit limited stability in aqueous environments, potentially compromising their structural integrity and fluorescence properties, thereby hindering their application in biological and environmental sensing. To address this, researchers have developed water-stable MOFs [9–11]. For instance, Zheng et al. [11] recently reported a water-stable terbium-based MOF fluorescence sensor for quantitative detection of cefixime. This sensor demonstrated a linear range of 0.1–150 µM and a detection limit of 31.55 nM.

Concurrently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest owing to their unique optical properties, high photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and low cost [18,19]. These dots can be synthesized from diverse sources [18,19]. For instance, Zhang et al. [18] utilized black soya beans as an inexpensive carbon source to synthesize fluorescent carbon quantum dots for cefixime detection, achieving a high sensitivity and an impressively low detection limit of 169 nM. In another study, Akhgari et al. [19] synthesized carbon dots from pomegranate juice using a hydrothermal technique. The resulting fluorescent carbon dots exhibited sensitive and selective cefixime detection through the inner filter effect, demonstrating a good linear relationship over a concentration range of 0.2 × 10–6 M to 8 × 10–6 M and achieving a detection limit of 0.5 × 10–7 M. Based on literature reports [18], both CDs and MOFs offer unique advantages for fluorescence sensing, but they also present specific challenges.

To address the limitations of water instability in MOFs and inconsistent fluorescence of conventional carbon dots, this study aims to develop a water stable and sensitive fluorescence sensor based on boron- and nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) integrated with UiO-66 MOF. The objective is to construct a nanocomposite (B,N-CD@UiO-66) that combines the high porosity and chemical robustness of UiO-66 with the enhanced optical properties of B,N-CDs, enabling rapid, selective, and ultra-sensitive detection of cefixime. The nanocomposite was synthesized via a simple solvothermal method and extensively characterized using BET, XRD, XPS, SEM, EDS, NMR, PL, and UV–Vis analyses to confirm its structure and functionality. This work further investigates the sensor’s detection performance in various real-world samples, highlighting its potential for applications in biomedical diagnostics, food safety monitoring, and environmental surveillance.

All reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and employed without further purification. Zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4, >99.5 %), 1,4-benzene dicarboxylic acid (BDC, 98 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ACS reagent, ≥97 %, pellets), and N, N-dimethylformamide anhydrous (DMF, 99.8 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (99.5 %) and boric acid (61.84 %) were purchased from British Drug Houses Co., Ltd (England). Urea (ACS reagent, 99 %) and hydrochloric acid (37 %) were acquired from Merck Co., (Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material synthesis: UiO-66 MOF, carbon dots and B,N-CD@UiO-66

ce:section.ce:para: UiO-66 MOF was synthesized following a modified solvothermal method reported in previous studies [20,21]. Briefly, zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4, 1.86 g) and 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC, 1.845 g) were dissolved in 60 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (37 %, 6 mL) was added to the solution as a modulator, influencing the nucleation and growth kinetics of the MOF crystals. The mixture was then vigorously stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 12 h in a muffle furnace. After cooling to room temperature, the product was isolated by centrifugation and washed three times each with DMF, ethanol, and distilled water. Finally, the obtained UiO-66 solid was dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boron, nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (B,N-CD)
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid-derived carbon dots were synthesized following a modified method reported in the literature [22]. Briefly, a mixture of 0.5 g citric acid, 0.5 g boric acid, and 0.25 g urea was dissolved in 50 mL of ethanol and stirred vigorously for 30 min at room temperature. The homogeneous solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a muffle furnace. After cooling to ambient temperature, the reaction mixture was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove unreacted reagents. The resulting solution was further purified by dialysis using a 75 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane against ethanol for 12 h. The purified carbon dot solution was then stored in the refrigerator.

The B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite was synthesized via an in-situ approach. 0.5 g of pre-synthesized UiO-66 was dispersed in an ethanolic solution containing 0.5 g citric acid, 0.5 g boric acid, and 0.25 g urea. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 30 min before being transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was thoroughly washed several times with an ethanol-water mixture and subsequently dried overnight at 70 °C under vacuum.

This in-situ synthesis strategy facilitates the growth of boron-doped carbon dots within the pores of the pre-formed UiO-66 MOF, resulting in intimate contact between the CDs and the MOF framework. This intimate contact maximizes the interfacial area and promotes strong interactions between the two components, potentially enhancing the stability and sensing performance of the resulting nanocomposite. To assess the impact of synthesis method on stability, a control experiment was performed by physically mixing B,N-CDs with pre-synthesized UiO-66 MOF. This mixture exhibited a rapid decline in fluorescence response within three days, attributed to weak interactions causing aggregation and quenching. In contrast, the in-situ synthesized B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite demonstrated exceptional stability, retaining its fluorescence for 60 days. This enhanced stability stems from strong electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions between the B,N-CDs and the UiO-66 framework, preventing leaching and degradation. Consequently, all subsequent studies were conducted using the in-situ synthesized B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite, highlighting its superior stability and practical potential as a reliable fluorescence sensor.

The quantum yields (ϕ) of the synthesized samples were determined using a comparative method, employing rhodamine B (RhB) as a standard (ϕ = 0.65 in ethanol) as per established procedures [23]. To minimize re-absorption and inner filter effects, the absorbance of all samples was maintained below 0.10. The ϕ was calculated using the following equation:

The synthesized materials were comprehensively characterized using a suite of analytical techniques. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using an FTIR-8700 spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, Japan) to obtain spectra in the 400–4000 cm⁻¹ range. Samples were analyzed as thin films on a silicon window, using a resolution of 4 cm⁻¹, and 5 scans were averaged using a deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector. Morphological analysis was conducted using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) on an FEI Inspect 550 microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) operating at 15 kV, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on an HT7700 microscope (Hitachi HighTech, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 40–120 kV.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a DongWoo fluorescence spectrometer (SC-100FS, South Korea) over a temperature range of 77 K to 297 K. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Specs-flex XPS Microprobe (SPECS, Germany) utilizing monochromatized Al Kα X-ray radiation (1486.71 eV). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Germany) equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm) in the 2θ range of 4–90°, with a scan rate of 0.02° s⁻¹, operating at 40 kV and 30 mA. UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi Fl4700 spectrophotometer.

All UV–Vis and fluorescence samples were prepared in distilled water as the solvent, unless otherwise stated. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were measured using a Micromeritics 3Flex instrument (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis was conducted to determine specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectra (¹H and ¹³C) were acquired using a Bruker DRX-500 MHz spectrometer (Billerica, MA, USA). DMSO‑d₆ was used as the solvent, and tetramethylsilane (TMS) served as the internal reference.

To prepare the B,N-CD@UiO-66 suspension, 3.0 mg of the nanocomposite was dispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.0). The suspension was then subjected to ultrasonic stirring for 30 min to ensure homogeneity. The fluorescence intensity of this stable suspension was measured as a control. For cefixime (CFX) detection, a series of CFX solutions with varying concentrations (0.1 nM to 150 µM) were prepared using deionized water. Subsequently, 25 µL of each CFX solution was added to 175 µL of the stable B,N-CD@UiO-66 suspension, followed by gentle swirling. After an incubation period of 5 min, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the range of 350 to 680 nm with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 430 nm was recorded after the emission stabilized. A standard curve was constructed by plotting the fluorescence intensity (Y-axis) against the corresponding CFX concentration (X-axis). All experiments were performed in quadruplicate, and the average of the data points was used for subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference and pH effect studies
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity and anti-interference capabilities of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite, potential interfering species commonly encountered in water samples, including antibiotics and metal ions, were investigated. Firstly, the influence of pH on the fluorescence response of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite was evaluated within the pH range of 2.9 to 10. Subsequent experiments were conducted at the optimal pH.

Aqueous solutions of various antibiotics (metronidazole, amoxicillin, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and cefuroxime) were prepared at a concentration of 1 mM. 30 µL of interfering antibiotic solution was then added to 170 µL of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 suspension containing 0.1 mM CFX. After 10 min of ultrasonic stirring, the fluorescence intensity of each mixture was measured.

Aqueous solutions of various metal ions (Cu2+, K +, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Fe2+) were prepared at a concentration of 1 mM using deionized water. Subsequently, 30 µL of interfering metal ion solution was added to 170 µL of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 suspension containing 0.1 mM CFX. After 10 min of ultrasonic stirring, the fluorescence intensity of each mixture was measured.

Control experiments were performed by adding the same volumes of antibiotic and metal ion solutions individually to the B,N-CD@UiO-66 suspension in the absence of CFX and mixture of interfering species with and without CFX was also investigated. All experiments were performed in quadruplicate to ensure the accuracy and reproducibility of the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing performance of B,N-CD@UiO-66 with real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the practical applicability of the present probe in real-world samples, seawater, tap water, urine, and honey samples were selected. Sample preparation procedures were adapted from previously reported methods [9–11,19,24,25].
•
Seawater, Tap Water: Seawater and tap water samples were directly analyzed without any pretreatment.
•
Honey: Honey samples were diluted fifty-fold with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). Subsequently, 0.2 µM of CFX was spiked into the diluted honey sample. The mixtures were then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatants were analyzed using fluorescence measurements.
•
Urine: Fresh urine samples were collected from a healthy individual and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to remove impurities. The supernatant was then diluted tenfold with PBS (pH 7.0). 0.2 µM of CFX was spiked into the diluted urine sample, followed by fluorescence measurements.

For all sample types, the fluorescence spectra of the samples without spiked CFX were initially recorded as blanks. The recovery (%) of CFX in each sample matrix and relative standard deviation (RSD%) were calculated to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the developed sensor using the following equations:

The probable formation reactions and interactions of UiO-66 MOF, B,N-CD, and the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite are proposed, drawing from existing literature and the chemical properties of the reactants:

ce:para.ce:bold: i. Hydrolysis and condensation of ZrCl₄

ce:section.ce:para: Zirconium chloride (ZrCl₄) undergoes hydrolysis and condensation in the presence of water (from DMF decomposition) to form Zr₆O₄(OH)₄ clusters, which serve as the inorganic nodes of UiO-66:

ZrCl4 + H2​O→Zr6​O4​(OH)4 ​+ HCl
•
DMF decomposes slowly at high temperatures, releasing small amounts of formic acid and water, which facilitate controlled hydrolysis of ZrCl₄.
•
The Zr₆O₄(OH)₄ cluster forms via oxo (Zr–O–Zr) and hydroxo (Zr–OH) bridges, leading to a highly stable, octahedral Zr-based secondary building unit (SBU).

ce:para.ce:bold: ii. Coordination of terephthalic acid (H₂BDC) to Zr₆ clusters

ce:section.ce:para: Terephthalic acid (H₂BDC) coordinates to the Zr₆O₄(OH)₄ clusters via bidentate carboxylate (-COO⁻) coordination, forming strong Zr–O–C bonds and stabilizing the 3D MOF structure:

Zr6​O4​(OH)4​ + 12H2​BDC→ UiO-66 + 4H2​O
•
Deprotonation of H₂BDC occurs due to the presence of an added modulator (HCl).
•
The carboxyl (-COOH) groups of H₂BDC donate electrons, forming covalent coordination bonds with Zr⁴⁺ sites in the Zr₆ cluster.
•
The resulting Zr–O–C bonds are strong, providing high thermal and chemical stability.

ce:para.ce:bold: Step 2: Formation of boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs)

ce:section.ce:para: • Citric acid (C₆H₈O₇) acts as a carbon precursor and undergoes dehydration and decarboxylation under hydrothermal conditions. The precursors dissolve in ethanol, forming a homogeneous reaction medium [26].
C6​H8​O7​ + H3​BO3​ + NH2​CONH2​ → Polymeric precursor complex

ii. Polymerization and nucleation (heating stage at 180 °C)
•
As the solution is heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave, citric acid undergoes thermal condensation and polymerization.
•
Boric acid reacts with hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups of citric acid, forming boron-carbon (B–C) and boron-oxygen (B–O–C) linkages.
•
Simultaneously, urea decomposes into NH₃ and HNCO, which introduce pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N, and amine-N functionalities into the carbon framework.
•
The polymeric intermediates form small nucleation sites, which gradually grow into carbon dots (CDs) through aromatization and carbonization as shown in Scheme S1.

ce:para.ce:bold: Step 3: Bonding and anchoring of B,N-CDs to UiO-66 to form B,N-CD@UiO-66

ce:section.ce:para:
As B,N-CDs form, they interact with UiO-66 through multiple bonding interactions, including (Scheme S1):
1.
Electrostatic interactions between the Zr⁴⁺ sites of UiO-66 and the negatively charged B,N-CD functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -NH₂).
2.
π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic rings of the B,N-CDs and the terephthalic acid (H₂BDC) linker of UiO-66.
3.
Hydrogen bonding between oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups of B,N-CDs and UiO-66, enhancing stability.

In summary, the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite is synthesized in two steps. First, B,N-codoped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) are prepared via hydrothermal treatment, incorporating B and N through B–C, B–O, B–N, and N–C bonds, which enhance fluorescence, hydrophilicity, and stability. Next, the B,N-CDs are integrated into the UiO-66 framework via an in-situ approach (Scheme 1 ), stabilized by electrostatic forces, hydrogen bonding, and π–π interactions. The resulting nanocomposite shows excellent stability, uniform dispersion, and enhanced fluorescence, making it suitable for selective cefixime sensing in biological and environmental samples.

The XRD pattern of the B,N-CD (Fig. 1 ) exhibited a broad, diffuse peak in the rage of 2θ = 10°–25°, characteristic of amorphous carbon materials. This lack of sharp, distinct peaks indicates a disordered arrangement of carbon atoms lacking long-range order. Two discernible peaks were observed at 2θ = 10.44° and 23.53° The peak at 2θ = 10.44° can be attributed to the interlayer spacing of turbostratic carbon domains or partially exfoliated graphitic layers [27,28], suggesting a larger d-spacing (0.75 Å) than typical graphitic materials. This increased d-spacing is likely due to structural distortions caused by the incorporation of boron and nitrogen within the carbon structure. The peak at 2θ = 21.53° (d-spacing of 3.75 Å), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, exhibited a shift compared to the typical (002) peak of pure graphite (around 25°) [27–29], further indicating the presence of defects or distortions within the graphitic structure. Overall, the XRD pattern confirmed the amorphous nature of the B,N-CD with the presence of graphitic-like domains.

The XRD pattern of UiO-66 MOF exhibited a series of sharp and distinct diffraction peaks, characteristic of its highly crystalline structure. Key diffraction peaks at low 2θ angles (around 7–10°) corresponded to long-range order within the UiO-66 framework, aligning with reflections from planes such as (111), (200), (220) and (600). The observed XRD pattern closely matched the simulated pattern (CCDC No. 837,796) [30], confirming the formation of the expected face-centered cubic (fcc) structure. Higher-angle peaks (e.g., 15–40°) originated from smaller periodicities within the framework, attributed to zirconium (Zr) clusters and the organic linkers. The absence of significant peak broadening or amorphous humps indicated high crystallinity with minimal structural defects. The calculated crystallinity percentage was 89.7 %, further supporting the high degree of crystallinity. The crystallite size, calculated using the Scherrer equation based on the most intense peak (111), was 89.95 nm. The observed diffraction pattern closely matched reported patterns for pristine UiO-66 MOF in the literature [30–35], confirming the successful synthesis of the material.

The XRD pattern of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite retained the characteristic diffraction peaks of UiO-66 MOF, including those corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (600) planes, confirming the preservation of the UiO-66 MOF framework. While the broad diffraction features typically observed at approximately 10.44° and 21.53° for B,N-CD were either merged with the background or exhibited reduced intensity, likely due to the dominance of the crystalline UiO-66 MOF peaks. A slight decrease in the intensity of some UiO-66 peaks, such as the (111) peak, was observed, potentially indicating partial interactions or binding between the B,N-CD and the MOF framework. The calculated crystallinity percentage decreased to 81.6 %, and the crystallite size increased to 96.9 nm, further supporting the interaction between the components. The absence of any new diffraction peaks in the composite pattern indicated that the formation of the nanocomposite did not lead to the creation of a new crystalline phase but rather a successful integration of the B,N-CD within the existing UiO-66 MOF framework.

Fig. 2 presents the FTIR spectra of B,N-CD, UiO-66 MOF, and the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite. The B,N-CD spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks of carbon-based materials. A broad peak observed in the range of 3300–3500 cm-1 was attributed to O-H stretching vibrations, likely arising from hydroxyl groups or adsorbed water molecules. Peaks around 1380–1600 cm-1 were assigned to C = C and C = O stretching vibrations in aromatic rings and carbonyl groups, respectively, indicating the presence of graphitic structures within the B,N-CD.

The UiO-66 MOF spectrum showed characteristic peaks of the terephthalic acid linker. A prominent peak at 1661 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the carboxylate group (COO-) in the linker. Peaks at 1587 cm-1 and 1386 cm-1 were assigned to C = C stretching and bending vibrations in the aromatic ring of the terephthalic acid linker, respectively. The sharp peak at 744 cm-1 was characteristic of the out-of-plane bending vibration of C-H bonds in the benzene ring. Peaks in the region of 450–800 cm-1 were attributed to Zr-O vibrations within the metal-organic framework [36].

The spectrum of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibited a combination of peaks observed in the individual spectra of B,N-CD and UiO-66 MOF, confirming the successful synthesis of the nanocomposite. Slight shifts in peak positions and changes in peak intensities were observed in the composite spectrum compared to the individual components, suggesting potential interactions between the B,N-CD and the UiO-66 MOF. Notably, a broadening of the O-H stretching peak in the nanocomposite spectrum might indicate hydrogen bonding interactions between the B,N-CD and UiO-66 MOF. Additionally, an increase in the intensity and broadening of the peak at 1587 cm-1 in the nanocomposite spectrum could be attributed to new bond formations between the B,N-CD and UiO-66 MOF. Overall, the FTIR analysis provided valuable insights into the functional groups present in each component and their potential interactions within the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite. The FTIR spectra of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibited characteristic peaks consistent with those reported for carbon dots/metal–organic framework composites in previous studies, such as that by Wu et al. [36].

XPS analysis of the full scan of B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite (Fig. 3 ) validated the successful incorporation of key elements, including C, B, N, Zr, and O, confirming the formation of the nanocomposite. The B 1 s and N 1 s peaks specifically indicate the incorporation of boron and nitrogen into the carbon dot structure, where the presence of B–N and C–N bonds highlights enhanced surface functionalization. This functionalization likely improves the dispersibility of the carbon dots and their interaction with the UiO-66 framework. The Zr 3d peaks, characteristic of the UiO-66 structures, confirm the effective integration of carbon dots with the MOF. Furthermore, the O 1 s peak provides additional evidence of the UiO-66 framework, with possible contributions from oxygen-containing functional groups in the B,N-CD.

The Zr 3d peak, deconvoluted into two peaks at 180.6 eV and 184.5 eV, corresponding to Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 spin-orbit splitting, confirmed the presence of zirconium within the UiO-66 framework [36,37]. The splitting energy between these peaks is approximately 3.9 eV, which is a known feature of Zr [38]. These binding energies are characteristic of Zr(IV) in the zirconium-oxygen bonds (Zr–O) of the UiO-66 MOF structure [38,39]. The deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum (280–290 eV) (Fig.S1) reveals various carbon bonding environments characteristic of doped carbon materials and composites [40,41]. The peak at ∼282.6 eV corresponds to graphitic or sp²-hybridized carbon (C=C), representing the graphitic backbone structure of the carbon dots or UiO-66 framework. The peak at ∼284.7 eV is attributed to carbon bonded to nitrogen (C–N), indicating nitrogen doping and the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the carbon lattice. Additionally, the peak at ∼288.5 eV corresponds to carbon in a carboxylate (O=C–O) or carbonyl (C=O) environment, commonly found in surface functional groups that facilitate interactions between the carbon dots and UiO-66 framework. These results confirm the successful nitrogen doping of carbon dots and their effective integration into the UiO-66 framework, contributing to enhanced functionality and potential applications.

The N 1 s spectrum (Fig.S1) was deconvoluted into two primary peaks, representing nitrogen in distinct chemical environments. The peak observed at 399 eV is attributed to pyridinic nitrogen, where nitrogen is bonded to two sp²-hybridized carbon atoms within a hexagonal carbon framework. In contrast, the peak at 401 eV corresponds to graphitic nitrogen, confirming the incorporation of graphitic nitrogen into the structure of the B,N-doped carbon dots [42,43]. The deconvoluted B 1 s spectrum reveals two prominent peaks. The peak at 192.2 eV corresponds to boron bonded to oxygen/nitrogen (B–O/N). In contrast, the peak at 194.6 eV is attributed to boron bonded to carbon (B–C) within a sp²-hybridized environment, indicating the incorporation of boron atoms into the carbon lattice by substituting carbon atoms and becoming part of the graphene-like structure. The incorporation of boron, particularly as B–C, plays a key role in enhancing the electronic properties of the carbon dots by introducing defects and modulating their electronic structure. A prominent O 1 s peak at around 530 eV was observed, attributed to oxygen from both the UiO-66 framework and functional groups within the B-CDs. These results collectively confirmed the successful synthesis of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite and verified the presence of all expected elements within the material. Collectively, FTIR, EDS, and XPS analyses confirmed the successful synthesis of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite and revealed the presence of all expected key elements within the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nuclear magnetic resonance (¹H and ¹³C NMR)
ce:section.ce:para: The 1H NMR spectra of B,N-CD and B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite (Fig.S2) reveal crucial structural insights and the impact of UiO-66 integration. The B,N-CD spectrum in DMSO‑d₆ (400 MHz) displays multiple peaks reflecting a diverse functional group composition. The aromatic region (δ 7.87–7.20 ppm) confirms the presence of aromatic rings, likely due to partial graphitization. Signals at δ 6.14–6.05 ppm suggest olefinic or pyrrolic protons, indicating heteroatom incorporation [26,44]. The oxygenated functional groups (-OH, -COOH, esters) are evident from signals in the δ 4.06–3.93 ppm range [45]. Additionally, aliphatic protons (CH, CH₂, CH₃) are observed between δ 3.00–1.09 ppm, confirming surface modifications. A distinct peak at δ 10.00 ppm suggests amine (-NH) or amide (-CONH) groups, indicative of nitrogen doping, crucial for enhancing electronic and optical properties.

In contrast, the 1H NMR spectrum of B,N-CD@UiO-66 exhibits a simplified spectral profile, suggesting strong interactions between B,N-CD and the UiO-66 framework. The aromatic proton signal at δ 8.03 ppm, attributed to the 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate (BDC) linker of UiO-66, confirms the retention of the MOF structure [46]. A singlet at δ 3.58 ppm corresponds to hydroxyl (-OH) or amine (-NH) groups, possibly from B,N-CD or UiO-66 interactions. A peak at δ 1.24 ppm suggests residual aliphatic protons. Notably, many characteristic signals of B,N-CD disappear or broaden, indicating restricted molecular motion and host-guest interactions within the MOF. The presence of DMSO (δ 2.50 ppm) and residual water (δ 3.38 ppm) is also observed. Overall, 1H NMR analysis confirms the successful integration of B,N-CD into UiO-66 via an in-situ approach, leading to a stable nanocomposite with distinct structural modifications.

The 13C NMR spectra of both B,N-CD and B,N-CD@UiO-66 MOF nanocomposite (Fig.S3) provide valuable insights into their structural characteristics and the effect of UiO-66 integration. The B,N-CD spectrum exhibits multiple peaks in the δ 160–170 ppm range, corresponding to sp2 -hybridized carbonyl and conjugated aromatic carbons, indicative of heteroatom doping with boron and nitrogen. Peaks at δ 101–102 ppm suggest graphitic carbon structures, while resonances around δ 40–41 ppm and δ 39–40 ppm correspond to sp3 carbon centers, likely from functional groups linked to nitrogen or boron. Additionally, a peak at δ 14.59 ppm indicates the presence of terminal methyl groups, and observed JJJ-coupling (21.0 Hz) suggests carbon-boron interactions.

In contrast, the B,N-CD@UiO-66 MOF spectrum exhibits significant spectral simplification. The absence or weakening of peaks in the δ 160–170 ppm region suggests strong interaction between B,N-CD and the UiO-66 framework, possibly through coordination or π-π interactions. The dominant peaks around δ 40.15–40.08 ppm correspond to UiO-66 linker carbon environments and functionalized sp3 carbons from B,N-CD. A minor peak at ∼129 ppm hints at residual aromatic carbon environments from the MOF linkers or retained graphitic domains. The observed spectral changes confirm the successful integration of B,N-CD within UiO-66, highlighting strong interactions between the carbon dots and the MOF structure, validating the effectiveness of the in situ synthesis approach in forming a well-integrated hybrid material.

The FE-SEM image (Fig. 4a ) shows UiO-66 MOF with a dense network of interconnected, polyhedral particles exhibiting characteristic octahedral morphology [47]. The agglomerated structure features rough surfaces and interparticle voids, indicating high porosity and surface area. Particle size analysis via ImageJ revealed sizes ranging from 5.85 to 22.8 nm, with an average of 15.3 nm, aligning with BET results. These observations confirm the formation of a robust, porous, and crystalline UiO-66 framework. The FE-SEM image (Fig. 4b) shows the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite with a well-dispersed, heterogeneous morphology. The UiO-66 framework appears as aggregated clusters with a rough, porous surface, while uniformly distributed B,N-CDs impart a granular texture, increasing surface roughness. This integration forms a hierarchical porous structure that may enhance electron transfer, suggesting synergistic interactions between B,N-CDs and UiO-66—potentially improving catalytic, sensing, and adsorption performance over pristine UiO-66.

The TEM image (Fig.S4a) shows uniformly dispersed, quasi-spherical B,N-CDs with minimal aggregation. The particles exhibit a narrow size distribution, predominantly below 10 nm, consistent with typical bottom-up synthesis methods. The clear contrast and defined edges confirm the electron-dense nature of the doped carbon cores, while the uniform shape and dispersion suggest good colloidal stability, likely due to surface functionalization from B and N co-doping. The absence of larger clusters indicates effective passivation and stabilization. No distinct lattice fringes are observed, indicating an amorphous or low-crystalline structure—common in heteroatom-doped CDs where B and N introduce defects and functional groups (e.g., B–O, B–N, C–N), disrupting graphitic order. This amorphous nature enhances surface states, which is beneficial for optical applications. In contrast, the TEM image of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite (Fig.S4b) revealed a more complex and heterogeneous morphology. The UiO-66 MOF framework remained evident, but the incorporation of B,N-CD resulted in a granular appearance due to the presence of finely dispersed B,N-CD particles. Some B,N-CD appeared embedded within the MOF pores, while others were located on the MOF surface. This intimate interaction between B,N-CD and the UiO-66 MOF led to the formation of a hierarchical structure with enhanced surface roughness. The observed morphology suggests a successful integration of B,N-CD within the UiO-66 MOF framework, resulting in a composite material with a complex and heterogeneous structure.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite (Fig. S4c) confirmed the presence of expected elements (C: 19.65 wt. %, O:36.46 wt. %; Zr:31.71 wt. %; B:3.63 % and N: 8.54 wt. %). A weak peak corresponding to boron (B) and nitrogen (N) in the low-energy region provided evidence for the successful doping of carbon dots. The presence of a strong peak at approximately 2.0 keV confirmed the presence of zirconium (Zr), a key component of the UiO-66 framework. A prominent peak at around 0.28 keV indicated the presence of carbon, originating from both the carbon dots and the terephthalic acid linker of the UiO-66 MOF. The relative intensities of these peaks aligned with the expected elemental composition of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface area and pore size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5a , the N₂ adsorption/desorption isotherm of UiO-66 displays a Type I profile, typical of microporous materials [40,48,49]. The sharp uptake at low relative pressure (p/p°) indicates abundant micropores (<2 nm) [47], attributed to the crystalline framework of UiO-66. BET analysis revealed a high surface area of 423.9 m²/g (Langmuir: 766.5 m²/g), with a micropore area of 274.1 m²/g and volume of 0.1345 cm³/g. The external surface area was 149.8 m²/g, total pore volume 0.2733 cm³/g, and average pore size 2.58 nm. The small particle size (14.15 nm) enhances surface accessibility, while a slight curvature at higher pressures suggests minor mesoporosity. The N₂ adsorption/desorption isotherm of B,N-CD shows a Type I profile, confirming microporosity. The sharp uptake at low relative pressures indicates the presence of micropores formed via small cavities and interconnected channels during synthesis. BET analysis reveals a surface area of 65.46 m²/g, with a micropore area of 20.39 m²/g and external surface area of 45.07 m²/g. The total pore volume is 0.0286 cm³/g, and the average pore size of 1.75 nm further confirms the microporous nature.

The N₂ adsorption/desorption isotherm of B,N-CD@UiO-66 shows a Type IV isotherm with a hysteresis loop, indicative of mesoporosity and ink-bottle-type pores. A sharp uptake at high relative pressure confirms mesopores (2–50 nm) [50–52], likely introduced by B,N-CD integration into the UiO-66 framework. This incorporation may generate voids or interconnect carbon dot networks, enhancing mesoporosity beyond that of pristine UiO-66. The nanocomposite exhibits a BET surface area of 35.63 m²/g, external surface area of 46.09 m²/g, pore volume of 0.03105 cm³/g, and an average pore size of 3.49 nm. The larger average particle size (168.4 nm) suggests some degree of aggregation upon composite formation.

Fig. 5b displays the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of B,N-CD, UiO-66 MOF, and the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite. The B,N-CD exhibited a strong emission peak centered at 440 nm, characteristic of carbon dots [53]. This blue emission is attributed to the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs within the sp2-hybridized carbon domains, influenced by surface states and defects introduced by nitrogen and boron doping. The PL spectrum of UiO-66 shows a broader emission in the range of 325–600 nm, attributed to its metal-organic framework structure, specifically the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) or the interaction between Zr-based nodes and the organic linkers. The red shift (456 nm) relative to B,N-CD indicates its different optical properties and electronic structure.

The B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite displays a PL spectrum incorporating features of both the B,N-CD and UiO-66 components. Notably, the nanocomposite exhibits a significant enhancement in PL intensity compared to its individual constituents. The emission spectrum reveals a broad peak in the blue-green region, red-shifted to 486 nm, indicative of strong electronic interactions or energy transfer between the B,N-CD and the UiO-66 framework. Upon incorporation into UiO-66, B,N-CDs may experience enhanced π-π stacking interactions or electronic coupling with the MOF framework. This can extend the electron delocalization, reducing the energy gap between the excited and ground states, leading to a redshift in fluorescence. Possible charge transfer mechanisms between B,N-CDs and UiO-66 could also contribute to this shift by modifying the electronic structure of the nanocomposite. This enhancement is attributed to synergistic effects, such as energy transfer and improved charge separation, facilitated by the interface between the two components.

The pronounced red-shift in emission suggests the involvement of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or similar mechanisms, reflecting modifications in the electronic states of the B,N-CD due to their integration within the UiO-66 matrix. These changes likely arise from enhanced coupling between the electronic states of the carbon dots and the MOF, optimizing their optical response. The increased fluorescence intensity and spectral shifts underscore the potential of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite for fluorescence-based applications, with improved sensitivity and selectivity for target analytes.

The UV–vis absorption and emission properties of the synthesized materials are shown in Fig.S5. The absorption spectrum of UiO-66 displays characteristic peaks in the UV region, attributed to LMCT transitions. In contrast, the B,N-CD spectrum reveals broad absorption bands spanning the UV-visible range, typical of carbon dots, which arise from π–π* transitions of sp²-hybridized domains and n–π* transitions associated with surface functional groups. The absorption spectrum of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite integrates features from both UiO-66 and B,N-CD, confirming the successful incorporation of B,N-CD into the MOF framework. The broad absorption across the UV-visible range suggests enhanced light-harvesting capabilities, making the nanocomposite particularly advantageous for fluorescence-based sensing applications.

The emission spectra of UiO-66 and B,N-CD were not analyzed, as they are not directly relevant to the sensing application. However, the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite demonstrates excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence (EWD) behavior, with the emission peak shifting from 455 nm to 430 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 250 nm to 330 nm. This behavior indicates the presence of multiple emissive centers within the nanocomposite [54–56]. The interaction between the B,N-CD and the UiO-66 framework may have resulted in the formation of new emissive states or modifications to the energy levels of the B,N-CD, giving rise to the observed EWD behavior.

Additionally, the UiO-66 MOF may play a role in stabilizing specific emissive states of the B,N-CD, thereby influencing the shift in emission wavelengths. The observed EWD behavior suggests that the nanocomposite's fluorescence response to cefixime could be modulated by the excitation wavelength, potentially involving excitation-dependent charge transfer or energy transfer processes. These mechanisms may arise from selective interactions between cefixime and specific surface groups on the nanocomposite, further enhancing its fluorescence sensing capabilities [54,55]. This tunability of the emission response offers flexibility in optimizing detection conditions for cefixime. Further investigations revealed that varying the excitation wavelength from 330 to 400 nm did not significantly shift the maximum emission peak of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite, which remained consistently at 430 nm. Subsequent studies were therefore conducted using an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

Fig.S5 presents visual observations of the samples under natural and UV light illumination. The B,N-CD exhibited a strong, whitish-blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, characteristic of carbon dots and attributed to quantum confinement effects and the influence of emissive surface states. The UiO-66 MOF exhibited a faint blue or pale violet fluorescence under UV excitation. The B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite, however, displayed a significantly brighter blue fluorescence with a subtle cyan shift compared to the individual components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and stability assessment in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibited a quantum yield of 32.9 %, indicating a decrease in fluorescence efficiency compared to the B,N-CDs. This reduction is likely due to surface passivation effects during integration with UiO-66, which can change the electronic states of the B,N-CDs and subsequently lower their quantum yield. To assess its suitability for long-term sensing applications, the optical stability of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite aqueous solution was evaluated over a 27-day period under two storage conditions: room temperature and refrigeration at 12 °C. Fig.S6a-b presents the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite over this period. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed on the first day for both storage conditions, indicating the initial fluorescence of the pristine nanocomposite. A gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed over the 27-day period under both storage conditions.

The fluorescence intensity decreased by 4.12 % at room temperature and by 2.23 % at 12 °C over the 27-day period (Fig.S6c). While a slight decrease in fluorescence was observed, the nanocomposite maintained a significant portion of its initial fluorescence intensity. These results demonstrate that the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibits reasonable optical stability over time, particularly under refrigerated conditions, suggesting its potential for long-term use in fluorescence-based sensing applications, such as the detection of antibiotics like cefixime. These findings are consistent with previous studies, such as that by Qin et al. [10], who reported only a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity for Cd-MOF/Tb3+ nanosheets after 7 weeks of dispersion in deionized water. To further validate its long-term stability, an extended 60-day trial was conducted, showing only a 2.5 % decrease in fluorescence intensity. This stability surpasses most reports in the literature, where fluorescence-based sensors are typically tested for only two weeks in aqueous solutions. Additionally, the solid-state B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite, stored for over six months, maintained 100 % of its fluorescence performance, highlighting its robustness. These findings confirm that the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite is highly stable and suitable for practical sensor applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of variation in solution pH
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite was investigated and the results are presented in Fig.S6d. The nanocomposite exhibited pH-dependent fluorescence, with the maximum intensity observed at pH 5.12, indicating optimal optical performance in slightly acidic conditions. Fluorescence intensity was lowest at pH 2.9, likely due to protonation of functional groups on both B,N-CD and the UiO-66 framework under strongly acidic conditions. This protonation may lead to non-radiative energy dissipation pathways or disrupt energy transfer between the B,N-CD and UiO-66, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Increasing the pH from 2.9 to 6.02 led to a significant increase in fluorescence intensity, peaking at pH 5.12. Further increasing the pH from 6.02 to 7.98 resulted in a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity, suggesting that a near-neutral pH environment is less favorable for fluorescence compared to the slightly acidic environment. This decrease might be attributed to deprotonation effects that alter the electronic states and energy transfer efficiency within the nanocomposite. At pH 9.18–10, fluorescence intensity further decreased, potentially due to deprotonation of surface groups, leading to aggregation or destabilization of the composite structure. Based on these findings, pH 5.12 was chosen as the optimal pH for subsequent sensing experiments, considering the nanocomposite's stability and its highest fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance: florescence sensing of cefixime and calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching of B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite by Cefixime (CFX) was investigated over two concentration ranges: 0–100 nM and 0–20 µM. As shown in Fig. 6a , a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing CFX concentration in both ranges, indicating an interaction between CFX and the nanocomposite. This quenching behavior suggests potential energy transfer or charge transfer processes, or interactions between functional groups in CFX (e.g., amino or carboxyl groups) and surface functionalities of the nanocomposite (e.g., boronic acid or amine groups). A more pronounced fluorescence quenching behavior was observed at lower concentrations of cefixime (CFX) compared to higher concentration ranges. To quantify the quenching efficiency, the Stern-Volmer equation [57,58] was applied:
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
×
[ Q ]
.
Where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of CFX, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the CFX concentration.

The Stern-Volmer plots show a good linear fit in both concentration ranges, with R2 values of 0.995 for 0–100 nM (Fig. 6b) and 0.989 for 0–20 µM. The KSV values were determined to be 3.51 M-1 for the lower concentration range and 0.0842 M-1 for the higher concentration range. The limit of detection (LOD), calculated as 3σ/KSV (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank suspensions over 10 measurements), was found to be 4.21 nM (1.907 ng/mL) for the lower concentration range and 17.5 nM for the higher concentration range. These LOD values highlight the exceptional sensitivity of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite for detecting CFX, with detection limits significantly lower than the maximum allowable concentrations of pharmaceuticals outlined in various regulatory guidelines, such as those from the EU and WHO. Under Directive 2008/105/EC, the EU has established environmental quality standards for certain pharmaceuticals, including a permissible concentration limit of 100 ng/L for ibuprofen in surface waters and a proposed Environmental Quality Standard of 40 ng/L for diclofenac to protect aquatic ecosystems. Similarly, the WHO provides guidelines on pharmaceuticals in drinking water, reporting concentration ranges of 10–60 ng/L, though specific maximum allowable limits for individual drugs are not explicitly defined [7,59].

A comparison with previously reported methods for CFX detection revealed that the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite-based sensor exhibited a lower limit of detection and a relatively wider linear range, as summarized in Table S1 [8,10,11,19,60]. This indicates improved sensitivity and a broader analytical window for CFX detection. Further analysis revealed that the quenching mechanism is likely static in nature. This is supported by the observation that the emission peak position of the nanocomposite remained unchanged upon the addition of CFX. Static quenching typically involves the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quencher, leaving the emission wavelength of the remaining unquenched fluorophores unaffected. At higher CFX concentrations, a slight deviation from linearity in the Stern-Volmer plot was observed. This suggests the contribution of additional quenching mechanisms, such as inner filter effects (absorption of excitation or emission light by the quencher) or secondary quenching processes (e.g., energy transfer between excited quencher molecules). To further investigate the quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed, the results of which are discussed in the subsequent sections.

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite-based fluorescence sensor were evaluated by assessing its response to various interfering substances, including other antibiotics and metal ions (listed in Section 2.4.2), in the presence of CFX. Fig. 7a presents the fluorescence spectra of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite in the presence of CFX and various interfering antibiotics: Amoxicillin (Amox), Tetracycline (Tet), Metronidazole (Metro), Ciprofloxacin (Cip), and Cefuroxime (Cefr).

The addition of CFX resulted in significant fluorescence quenching of the nanocomposite. While other antibiotics also induced some degree of quenching, Metro exhibited the most pronounced quenching effect. This observation suggests that Metro interacts more strongly with the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite compared to other antibiotics. The presence of the nitro group in the Metro structure, with its electron-withdrawing nature, likely facilitates stronger electronic interactions with the excited states of the fluorophore, leading to enhanced non-radiative decay and fluorescence quenching [61]. Additionally, the smaller and more planar structure of Metro may allow for more efficient access and interaction with the porous framework of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 compared to bulkier cephalosporins like CFX and Cefr.

Control experiments conducted with individual antibiotics in the absence of CFX (Fig. S7a) revealed that Metro alone exhibited a slightly higher fluorescence intensity compared to its presence in the presence of CFX. This observation suggests that when both Metro and CFX are present, CFX may partially displace Metro from its binding sites on the nanocomposite. Since Metro is a more effective quencher, this displacement results in reduced overall quenching compared to the case with Metro alone. Despite the presence of these interfering antibiotics, both individually and in a mixture (Fig. S7b), the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibited minimal interference in the detection of CFX, demonstrating good selectivity.

Fig. 7b demonstrates the effect of various interfering metal ions (Ag+, Ni2+, Fe2+, K +, Cu2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Fe3+) on the fluorescence detection of CFX using the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite. The addition of CFX alone resulted in the most significant fluorescence quenching, indicating a strong and specific interaction between CFX and the nanocomposite. This is likely attributed to the presence of the β-lactam ring in CFX, known for its reactivity, and the potential for hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between the carboxylate (-COO⁻) and amine (-NH₂) groups of CFX and the functional groups within the B,N-CD@UiO-66 framework. While most metal ions caused moderate quenching, Ag+ exhibited the least significant interference. Redox-active metal ions such as Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Mn2+ may facilitate non-radiative decay pathways, leading to fluorescence quenching. In contrast, alkali and alkaline earth metals (K +, Ca2+, Mg2+) generally exhibited weaker interactions due to their lower coordination affinity. Control experiments with metal ions alone (Fig. S7c) revealed similar trends, confirming that while metal ions can quench the fluorescence of the nanocomposite, they do not significantly interfere with the selective detection of CFX. This suggests that CFX maintains a strong and preferential binding affinity with the nanocomposite, minimizing the impact of potential metal ion interference.

To evaluate the influence of salinity on the sensor's performance, the fluorescence response of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite towards CFX was investigated in the presence of varying concentrations of NaCl (0.1 M to 50 M). As shown in Fig. 8a , the addition of CFX alone resulted in significant fluorescence quenching. However, the presence of NaCl led to varying degrees of fluorescence intensity changes. At lower NaCl concentrations (0.1 M to 0.5 M), a slight increase in fluorescence intensity was observed compared to the CFX-only condition, suggesting a reduction in CFX-induced quenching. This phenomenon may be attributed to the shielding effect of NaCl on the electrostatic interactions between CFX and the nanocomposite, such as those involving the carboxylate groups of CFX and the functional groups within the B,N-CD@UiO-66 framework.

However, at higher NaCl concentrations (1.0 M to 50 M), a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed. This decrease is likely due to the competitive binding of Na+ and Cl- ions with CFX or the nanocomposite within the porous structure of UiO-66, thus hindering the effective interaction between CFX and the nanocomposite. These results demonstrate that while the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibits moderate fluorescence quenching for CFX in low-salinity environments, its sensitivity is affected by high salt concentrations. Future studies will focus on strategies to enhance the salt tolerance of the sensor, such as incorporating hydrophobic or salt-resistant groups onto the nanocomposite surface.

The recyclability of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite sensor was evaluated by subjecting it to five consecutive sensing cycles. After each cycle, the nanocomposite was recovered by centrifugation, washed with ethanol, and dried at 70 °C before being reused. As illustrated in Fig. 8b, a slight enhancement in fluorescence quenching was observed in the initial cycles (second and third), potentially attributed to a slight rearrangement of the B,N-CD within the UiO-66 framework, leading to improved accessibility of CFX binding sites. However, subsequent cycles (fourth and fifth) exhibited a gradual decrease in fluorescence quenching. This decline in sensor performance could be attributed to several factors, including the adsorption of impurities from the solution during repeated use, which can interfere with fluorescence, or potential degradation or structural changes within the B,N-CD or the UiO-66 framework. These findings suggest that the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibits good reusability over three cycles, with a slight performance enhancement in the initial cycles, followed by a gradual decline in subsequent cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cefixime detection in practical matrices: Urine, honey, and tap water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of the as-synthesized B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite fluorescent probe, real-world samples (honey, tap water, and seawater) were spiked with known concentrations of CFX. Real-world samples, such as honey and biological fluids, often contain proteins and sugars that can interfere with fluorescence-based sensing by acting as quenching agents or through matrix effects, leading to inaccurate cefixime quantification. To mitigate these interferences, samples in this study underwent pretreatment, including 0.22 µm filtration and dilution, prior to analysis.

As presented in Table S2, the developed method demonstrated excellent accuracy and precision in the analysis of spiked samples. The recovery rates ranged from 96.9 % to 101.63 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranging from 1.54 % to 5.34 % (n = 4). To further validate the analytical performance, the CFX concentrations in spiked samples were also determined using a standard UV–Vis spectrophotometric method. The results obtained from both methods showed good agreement, confirming the accuracy and reliability of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite-based fluorescence sensor for the determination of CFX in complex matrices.

As noted in Section 3.7, the emission peak position of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite remained unchanged upon the addition of CFX, indicating a static quenching mechanism. This suggests the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between the B,N-CD@UiO-66 and CFX, likely mediated by weak intermolecular forces such as hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions. Consistent with this, fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig.S8a) revealed a negligible change in the average lifetime upon CFX addition (4.37 ns for the nanocomposite alone and 4.40 ns in the presence of CFX), further supporting the dominance of static quenching [57,58].

The involvement of Inner Filter Effects (IFE) was also evidenced [10]. While the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CFX exhibits some overlap with the emission spectrum of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite (Fig.S8b), the overlap is limited, suggesting that the inner filter effect might not be the dominant quenching mechanism. The observed deviation from linearity in the Stern-Volmer plot at higher CFX concentrations still points towards some contribution of IFE. However, fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. S8a) revealed a negligible change in lifetime upon CFX addition, indicating that static quenching plays a more significant role in the overall quenching process. XRD analysis revealed some significant changes in the crystal structure of the B,N-CD@UiO-66 nanocomposite upon the addition of CFX (Fig.S8c), further corroborating the presence of ground-state complex formation in the quenching mechanism. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of B,N-CD@UiO-66 by CFX is primarily attributed to a combination of static quenching (major) and inner filter effects (minor).

This study reports the successful development of a novel and highly sensitive fluorescence sensor for the detection of cefixime (CFX), utilizing B,N-codoped carbon dot@UiO-66 metal-organic framework (MOF) nanocomposites. Structural confirmation was achieved through a suite of techniques. XRD analysis verified the crystalline integrity of the UiO-66 framework after nanocomposite formation, while XPS confirmed the presence and successful doping of boron and nitrogen into the carbon dot lattice. FTIR spectroscopy identified characteristic functional groups from both B,N-CD and UiO-66, with spectral shifts and intensity changes indicating successful integration and interactions—such as potential hydrogen bonding—within the nanocomposite structure. TEM and SEM imaging revealed a uniform dispersion of B,N-CDs within the UiO-66 matrix, further supporting effective structural incorporation.

The resulting nanocomposite exhibited outstanding sensing performance, including a low limit of detection (4.21 nM), wide linear detection range (0–100 nM), and rapid response time (4.4 s). The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to a combination of static quenching and inner filter effects. The sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity against competing antibiotics and metal ions and showed high stability, retaining over 97 % of its fluorescence intensity after 60 days. Additionally, it was reusable across multiple cycles and effective in real-world sample matrices (honey, tap water, seawater) with high recovery rates. These findings highlight B,N-CD@UiO-66 as a robust and multifunctional platform for practical applications in environmental monitoring, food safety, and clinical diagnostics, offering a reliable and sensitive approach for detecting antibiotic residues such as cefixime.
